neutral.summary: City council voted 7-4 to approve a new 500-unit housing development in the downtown district, including 100 affordable units.

left.take: A step forward, but 20% affordable housing is not nearly enough when rent is crushing working families. We need bolder action on the housing crisis.

quotes.text: Housing is a human right, not a luxury for the wealthy.
quotes.speaker: Councilwoman Sarah Martinez

quotes.text: This project will drive up rents in neighboring areas and displace long-time residents.
quotes.speaker: Community Organizer Rosa Chen

quotes.text: We should be requiring 40% affordable units, minimum. Developers can afford it.
quotes.speaker: Housing Rights Attorney Mark Davis

quotes.text: Luxury condos don't solve the housing crisis. We need social housing like they have in Vienna.
quotes.speaker: Urban Planning Prof. Lisa Wong

right.take: Great news for property owners and the tax base. The free market works when we get out of the way and let developers build.

quotes.text: This project will create jobs and boost our economy without burdening taxpayers.
quotes.speaker: Mayor Tom Williams

quotes.text: More housing supply means lower prices. Economics 101.
quotes.speaker: Developer John Mitchell

quotes.text: This kind of private investment revitalizes downtowns and creates tax revenue for schools and services.
quotes.speaker: Chamber of Commerce President Linda Hayes

quotes.text: Property rights matter. If someone wants to build on their land, government shouldn't micromanage.
quotes.speaker: Councilman Robert Price

quotes.text: This development will bring young professionals downtown and support local businesses.
quotes.speaker: Real Estate Analyst Karen Smith
